g.data-narration: Welcome to Java Thread Model - a comprehensive guide to multithreading in Java.
A thread is a lightweight subprocess that allows concurrent execution within a program for better CPU utilization.
g.text: for better CPU utilization and responsiveness.

g.data-narration: Multithreading enables parallel task execution, responsive UIs, simplified program structure, and optimal CPU usage.
A Java thread can be in one of six states: New, Runnable, Blocked, Waiting, Timed Waiting, or Terminated.
g.text: States: New → Runnable → Blocked/Waiting/Timed Waiting → Terminated

g.data-narration: Blocked threads wait for monitor lock, Waiting threads wait indefinitely, and Timed Waiting threads wait for a specified period.
g.text: These are non-runnable states where thread cannot proceed until condition changes.

g.data-narration: Thread state transitions: start() moves New to Runnable, blocking/waiting methods move to blocked states, and run() completion ends in Terminated.
Method 1: Extend the Thread class and override the run() method. Then create instance and call start() to begin execution.
g.text: ⚠️ Note: Always use start(), not run() to begin thread execution

g.data-narration: Method 2: Implement Runnable interface and pass to Thread constructor. Preferred approach for flexibility.
g.text: ✓ Preferred: Allows multiple inheritance, easier resource sharing

g.data-narration: Thread priority ranges from 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX), default is 5 (NORM). Higher priority threads get more CPU time.
Synchronization prevents race conditions. Use synchronized keyword on methods or blocks to ensure thread-safe access to shared resources.
g.text: count++; // Only one thread at a time
⚠️ Prevents race conditions by ensuring only one thread accesses shared data at a time

g.data-narration: Use wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() for interthread communication. These methods allow threads to cooperate and share resources efficiently.
g.text: All three methods MUST be called from within a synchronized context

g.data-narration: Use isAlive() to check if thread is running, isInterrupted() to check interrupt status, and isDaemon() to check if thread is a background thread.
Every Java application has a main thread that executes the main() method. Get reference using Thread.currentThread().
g.text: • Every Java app has a main thread

g.data-narration: Runnable interface is preferred over Thread class because it allows multiple inheritance and easier resource sharing among threads.
Remember: Use start() not run(), prefer Runnable over Thread, use synchronized for thread safety, and master interthread communication with wait/notify.
